rulings.Case Title: MAHMUT YUKSEL ET AL VS. AMERICAN INTERNATIONAL INDUSTRIES, INC., ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, July 9, 2024, in Department 301, Line 3. Defendant American International Industries' Motion for Summary Adjudication is GRANTED, in part, and DENIED, in part. Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication of the Third Cause of Action is GRANTED. The elements of a cause of action for fraud are: (1) a misrepresentation, which includes a concealment or nondisclosure; (2) knowledge of the falsity of the misrepresentation, i.e., scienter; (3) intent to induce reliance on the misrepresentation; (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) resulting damages. (Small v. Fritz Companies, Inc. (2003) 30 Cal.4th 167, 173.) The same elements comprise a cause of action for negligent misrepresentation, except there is no requirement of intent to induce reliance. (Ibid.) Plaintiffs failed to put forth evidence of any specific misrepresentation of a material fact made by Defendant. Mr. Yuksel testified that he never purchased any Clubman product directly from Defendant and does not recall ever seeing any advertisement for either Clubman product he used. (UMF 22.) He never spoke to or had any verbal communication with an employee or agent of Defendant. (UMF 15.) Mr. Yuksel had no knowledge that Defendant ever made a misrepresentation to him or concealed any information from him, (UMF, 18), and he never received any written communication from an employee or representative of Defendant. (UMF 16.) Plaintiffs' responses to Special Interrogatories do not describe any misrepresentation. (Exh. M.) Likewise, Plaintiffs did not produce any documents showing misrepresentations from Defendant. Plaintiffs failed to show a disputed issue on this cause of action. Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication of the Fourth Cause of Action is DENIED. Generally, nondisclosure or concealment may constitute actionable fraud when the defendant is in a fiduciary relationship with the plaintiff. (See, Bigler-Engler v. Breg, Inc. (2017) 7 Cal.App.5th 276, 311.) When a fiduciary duty does not exist, a duty to disclose arises only "when the defendant had exclusive knowledge of material facts not known to the plaintiff," or "when the defendant actively conceals a material fact from the plaintiff," or "when the defendant makes partial representations but also suppresses some material facts." (Id. at 311.) This type of relationship " 'can only come into being as a result of some sort of transaction between the parties' " and "must necessarily arise from direct dealings between the plaintiff and the defendant; it cannot arise between the defendant and the public at large." (Ibid.) Evidence that the defendant was involved in retail sales of the disputed product to consumers and profited from them can satisfy the Bigler requirement. (Bader v. Johnson & Johnson (2022) 86 Cal.App.5th 1094, 1132.) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2.)

rulings.Case Title: MAHMUT YUKSEL ET AL VS. AMERICAN INTERNATIONAL INDUSTRIES, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT AMERICAN INTERNATIONAL INDUSTRIES MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 2) (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry.) Here, Defendant has only shown that Plaintiffs had no direct contact with Defendant. Defendant has not shown, through its evidence, that it was not involved in retail sales of the products and did not profit from those retail sales. On the contrary, the testimony shows that the Clubman products were for sale at Nordstrom and Macy's (where Plaintiff purchased them). Accordingly, Defendant did not show that Plaintiffs cannot reasonably obtain evidence of Defendant's involvement in retail sales of the products and profit from those sales, giving rise to a duty to disclose. Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication of Punitive Damages is DENIED. For a corporate defendant, the oppression, fraud or malice "must be on the part of an officer, director, or managing agent of the corporation." (Civ. Code, Section 3294, subd. (b).) However, a finding of malice against a corporation is appropriate when the malicious behavior is "corporate policy," rather than the behavior of a rogue employee whose conduct was unknown to those with management authority. (Cruz v. Home Base (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 160, 167-68.) So long as the offending conduct is authorized by the corporation, that corporation may be liable for punitive damages without any direct communication with the injured party. (Bullock v. Philip Morris USA, Inc. (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 655, 767-77.) Here, Plaintiffs present a triable issue of material fact that Defendant knew of the hazards of asbestos in its Clubman products and failed to issue warnings. Plaintiffs allege it was Defendant's offending corporate policies that led Defendant to fail to adequately warn of the hazards of their products and continue the manufacture and sale of such products after obtaining knowledge of the hazards of asbestos. (Exh. M; Index of Exhibits, Exh. D.) Plaintiffs' objection to Exhibit E is sustained as an incomplete document. (Evid. Code Section 356.) Defendant is correct that it has not been sued in its successor capacity. However, how that will impact the extent of Defendant's liability is an issue for the trial judge. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 301 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on July 9, 2024 at 9:30 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. =(301/RCE)

rulings.Case Title: THEODORE HALADY ET AL VS. GENERAL ELECTRIC COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, July 9, 2024, in Department 301, Line 4. Plaintiff's Motion for Leave to File First Amended Complaint is GRANTED under C.C.P. section 473(a)(1). No opposition filed. Plaintiff shall file and serve the First Amended Complaint forthwith on the newly added defendant K-M INDUSTRIES HOLDING CO. INC. (previously sued as Doe 66), and nominal defendants David Halady and Lorraine Haslehurst, as well as on those defendants who have not yet appeared in the case. The First Amended Complaint is deemed served on all other Defendants who have previously appeared in the action. It is further ordered that Delia Halady, Michael Halady and Theodore Halady Jr. be added as wrongful death heirs and plaintiffs to this action. The prevailing party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 301 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on July 9, 2024 at 9:30 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. =(301/RCE)
